Welcome everyone. Happy Wednesday everybody. Hello. Hello everybody. Well, we are here again. Greetings. Welcome. This is a session with boss vouchers. He is our CEO and a bestselling author. Today he is going to dive deep and explore social proof. As always, please have your microphones muted unless you are called on. Feel free to use the chat area to send any questions. Just raise your hand by using the feature and the reactions at the bottom. And without further ado, boss, please take us into some social proof. Thank you, Eileen. And great to see you all. Happy to be here. And indeed, we're going to dive into the principle of social proof today. And I love the principle of social proof because nowadays, of course, basically the whole world knows social proof and they're all guided by social proof when online came online reviews became a huge form of social proof. But that doesn't mean that social proof wasn't out there before online. So we will dive also in a bit of history and how social proof affected probably all of your choices. And to start talking about social proof, I want to show you a clip pre-internet. It's from Monty Python's and I know it's one of the favorite movies from one of the participants here. Life of Brian and here's a clip in and it shows how individual we all are. Let's take a look. I've got one or two things to say. Tell us, tell us what's wrong. Look, you've got it all wrong. You don't need to follow me. You don't need to follow anybody. You've got to think for yourself. You're all individuals. Yes, we are all individuals. You're all different. Yes, we are all different. I'm not. Well, so funny movie to start with, but it's the power of the crowd and that's what social proof is all about. When we, especially when we are uncertain, we decide what to do by what others do. What I first like to address is some common mistakes when people try to use social proof, because often I see. Boss, we lost, we lost you for just a second. I was kicked out and came back right away. So, okay, no worries. We're still here and good to go. So what I was sharing some common mistakes which I see very often people trying to use social proof. One is, for example, they share, well, 35% of the people are doing it and they think that is social proof and it helps their case. Actually, what you're saying is 65% of the people doesn't do your desired behavior. So, subconsciously, that's the message. So if you don't frame the majority in your, majority in your percentages, it will work against you. And that's a very common mistake. And it also has to do, and I think the best example is COVID. Governments were instructing people stay at home. Don't go out together. And then in the newspapers, in a lot of countries, they presented pictures, oh, people still hang out in the parks or they come together for certain parties. That became the behavior that was suggested as people are still doing it. So again, it backfires and it works against you. So, it's not only in your written statements, but also look at the images you use. What are people doing in those images? Because that's what happened with COVID. They just presented those images. Of course, they wrote an article. But as we all know, we process images much faster than text. So just by seeing those, that became like a standard. A lot of people later on commented when they were interviewed, why are you here in the park? You are instructed not to be here. Well, I saw in the newspapers everybody was doing it. So I decided I could do so as well. So social proof when it's used not in the right way, it can backfire. So I think that's the first thing what we need to be aware of when we are using social proof for ourselves or for our clients. So there are even people who book their honeymoon based on social proof. And maybe it sounds weird, but I think thinking about it, you are probably super uncertain when you want to go on a honeymoon. Most people do it once, some twice, three, four times, but let's assume most people do it once. So you never done it. You can go to the entire world and you want it to be super special. So indeed uncertainty and on TripAdvisor, there's a whole section in their website about honeymoons. You can get a lot of information what is most booked by certain groups like you. So newlyweds, are you in your 20s? Are you in your 40s? So they have this whole section and it's completely designed by social proof. Social proof is the guidance how to book your honeymoon. Maybe you didn't book your honeymoon based on social proof, but I definitely know we all ever selected a restaurant to eat based on social proof. If it wasn't online and pre-internet, if we went on a city trip to a city we never visited, how did we select the restaurant to go eat in? Was it the empty restaurant or was it the restaurant that was fully booked? Probably you decided the last one because when everybody is here, it must be better than the empty restaurant. You had no idea, so that was the shortcut that you followed. And nowadays a form of social proof which guides a lot of people in making choices is reviews. And in this example, it's again from TripAdvisor, but you find them basically everywhere from Amazon to a local service provider. Everybody nowadays tries to present what other people thinking of their product or their service. And we look at the amount of reviews they matter. And of course the score matters. And we will come to that in a second designing the perfect review statement. And I have some tips there. So before we dive into this, I want to show you two video clips. And for people that were like the majority here in order of teaching calls for me might hear that example, me explaining it. Today I want to show you the real original video clip. How powerful social proof is and why it's so powerful. The first video clip is from the Ash experiment. It's a very famous experiment. People have to compare lines and say what is the equal length of the other line. And you will see what happens. And then comes Dr. Chaldeany himself with another explanation. So let's dive into the first exam of the first video clip, the Ash experiment. The experiment you will be taking part in today involves the perception of lengths of lines. As you can see here I have a number of cards and on each card there are several lines. Your task is a very simple one. You are to look at the line on the left and determine which of the three lines on the right is equal to it in length. Alright we will proceed in this order. Only one of the people in the group is a real subject, the fifth person with a white t-shirt. The others are confederates of the experimenter and have been told to give wrong answers on some of the trials. The experiment begins uneventfully as subjects give their judgments. Two, two, two, two, three, three, three, three, three, three. But on the third trial something happens. Two, two, two, two, two. The subject denies the evidence of his own eyes and yields to group influence. Ash found subjects went along with the group on 37% of the critical trials. But he found through interviews that they went along with the group for different reasons. One, one, they must be right. They are four of them and one of me. One, this subject's yielding is based on a distortion of his judgment. He genuinely believes that the group is correct. He believes the group is correct instead of his own eyes. Which is, which was quite shocking actually at the time. So this became a very famous behavioral science experiment. This experiment is repeated and with lines but also with other things. Do people trust their own eyes or the crowd? And there were even higher numbers reached than 37%. Now I show you a video clip of Dr. Chaldey referring back to this original experiment. And what he has to say and here comes the explanation. Why social proof is so powerful and I found is very fascinating. I am taking a special interest in neuroscience, the neuroscience of influence and persuasion. So for example, there's one classic study in social psychology called the Ash study. In which people go around a table and choose between three lines which is the one they think is the longest. And the researchers have rigged the data so that the first five people who are all confederates of the experimenter say the wrong line. And then they get to the sixth person and they find a very high percentage of those six people, that sixth person will go along with the crowd instead of believing their own eyes. It's a clear difference perceptually. They replicated that study. Since then, a new study has been done to replicate that procedure with people who were hooked up to brain imaging devices. And what these researchers found was that when people came upon the situation where they were out of sync with their fellows there in the room, two things happened in their brain. One is the area associated with puzzlement lit up. And so did the area associated with physical pain. Being out of step with the group was physically painful for those individuals. Now, here's the brilliant thing about the study. They also did it with being out of step with five computers. What the computer said, you got puzzlement lighting up in your brain, but no pain. That's why it's so difficult for us to be out of step with the crowd, with our group, with our subculture, because it's painful to be the social consequences of being out of step are painful to us. There are no social consequences of being out of step with computers, but there are social consequences of being out of step with your friends, and it registers as pain. I thought that was a very important insight from that brain imaging study. So, some people of you might hear me sharing their story, but now you hear it from the master, the godfather himself. So, but it's fascinating, right? So, it's not only guidance for us when we are uncertain, but if there's an established group behavior, it's even painful not to take, not to be a participant of that group behavior, to go against the group. So, that leads us to two things. One, what we all associate nowadays with social proof is reviews. But these are mostly informative. Yes, the majority likes this product, of course, but what this also applies is, we need to tell people what is the group behavior in certain areas, like the customer journey or whatever behavior we like to target, because that causes pain if I don't do it. It's not only what the majority does, which takes social proof, when you are uncertain, but it's also painful to go against that behavior that is presented as the group behavior. So, social proof, and that's the good news of the day, is actually quite simple. We only have to do two things. And as you know, we can activate social proof by evidence, the proof itself, of what others think, feel and do. And yes, reviews is one way, and we will discover other ways today in this call, also together. And we will dive into reviews, because some elements you know what is important and what is most persuasive, but some elements we never share until now. So, that's what you need to do, share the evidence. And one of the companies in the world that embraced all principles of Dr. Cialdini, and I have some funny insight stories of, is Booking.com. By the way, one thing pops up, and I don't know if we ever shared it, the term social proof. Do you know that actually a term that Dr. Cialdini created, the first he named the principle Consensus, but it was, he started calling it social proof, and now it's a global known term, social proof. But it's actually the first name to principle, the principle of Consensus. So it's a term created by Dr. Cialdini, which is quite a cool story that you create a term and, well, the whole world now knows about it and uses it. So one of the companies that use it really well, some fun stories, because my co-author of my book, he was hired by Booking for the last four years. So I have some funny insight stories which are also allowed to share. One of those stories is actually in the updated version of Influence with Dr. Cialdini, because he joined in one of the calls with the head of CRO, Convergera Optimization, for Booking.com. The story is about scarcity, but what happened? They start reading about Dr. Cialdini's principles and say, wow, this is powerful. So this was in the beginning days of Booking.com. And they say, we have to test these principles. So they added scarcity for the first time to their website. And they were thinking the systems are broken. We see way too many bookings. It cannot be true. Eventually the systems were correct. It was just scarcity doing its work. It had a major update. But then they thought, well, he has more principles. Let's start working with that. And nowadays they are, of course, well known as a very persuasive company. But also ethically, I know they are corrected, and I will address that. But I also know they have a very intelligent system which produces this data. So these reviews are real. They don't make them up. How many people are looking at a certain hotel? They don't make it up. This is all automated. And it's even that sophisticated system. Every employee in Booking.com can set up an A-B test. So a test to improve conversion. So even, and that doesn't happen, of course, every day, but even the cleaning person could set up an A-B test. And they really are focused on everybody gets motivated to run certain tests. And the system is that smart that if your test produces a decrease in revenue, it stops the test right away. So sometimes tests only run for a few minutes, and then the system already sees the data because they have so many visitors. So how do they use social proof? And why I put this example in here, because simply telling what people do, there are so many forms of it. So I wanted to inspire you thinking, well, what can I show? And then I have a fun story about what people actually thinking and saying to Booking.com. And what was the real truth? So they say, and I did a, if I go to Booking, I get a Dutch page. So I translated it here. So they say, well, the hotel is fantastic. The location gets a 9.5 out of 10 and 6361 people reviewed it. There are currently five people watching this specific hotel. And it's booked today already 19 times. So that was still fair. And this data is real. What they were corrected on was scarcity. You say, see here in red, it's high in demand. Only three rooms left on our website. So the first correction they got, previously they said only three rooms left. They're on their side because hotels assign certain sites, a certain amount of rooms to them, and they keep also certain amount. So they have to correct that. And recently, but I have to read that article, there was still another one. I don't know if you are aware, Eilie, if you read that article, I saw it in a newspaper, but they get another correction where they overused the principles again. So yeah, so ethical balance again here is important because they of course get big fines nowadays because they are a big company, but also you lose a lot of credibility with your clients. So still important because you see it backfires. And when they get these big fines, you see equity, the equity value drops by the way. But what is a fun fact about social proof that clients were saying to booking.com, please stop sharing this information, how much time, how many times a hotel was booked, how many people are watching, you absolutely are not persuading me, that information, I only think it's annoying. So as what I just said, booking had a very sophisticated testing system, so they put it to the test. And instantly they saw a drop in amount of booking 30 to 35% by removing this social proof. So that's also suggest the power of doing it. So that's an example on how there are multiple forms of social proof and that's important to think about. That's why I started with review, but you can make so many quotes, what are people doing, how many people are doing certain behavior. And I think there's the magic to become a slew of influence and really stand out to get the maximum effect out of that principle, using it very ethically, just presenting information that's already available. And through systems nowadays, you don't even have to have very expensive software to collect that data. And another thing, social proof works for every age. It's deeply rooted in our system. And this is an experiment, what they did, it's where children that were afraid of dogs, they were afraid. And what they did, they let those children watch, it was toddlers by the way, so young children, toddlers, and they let the children watch a video 20 minutes a day of other toddlers playing with dogs and they were super happy playing with the dogs. Only after four days, but only four times 20 minutes, they watched a video of other toddlers playing with dogs. 67% lost the fear of dogs. So it doesn't only work on all ages, but this is also a very interesting way of how you can send out messages nowadays on social media, thinking of warming up your audience. What is the majority of your audience is doing, of your audience, of your clients. Because this was a pretty short, intervening interaction, it was only four days. Of course nowadays we don't get 20 minutes attention from everybody online, but it's by repeatedly make that social proof and raise it to awareness that could definitely flip the complete view of how people perceive how others are working with you, appreciate your servers, appreciate your product. And I don't see that happening often online. So that's another tip that I wanted to share. Then I said today, it's basically super simple social proof, two steps. The first step is we need the evidence. Now with AI, you have all kinds of AI tools that can find the statistics easily. What is most bought, most popular, what are the most people clicking on on your website in Google Analytics, you can find that data. Why the other words, produce a critic and advice produce a partner, but there's still another thing what also is proven by scientific research. If it's connected to reviews, people are much more likely to give you the review if you ask for advice, because who do you ask advice from. That's somebody who you think you can learn something from. So basically, you also give them some authority. If you ask for advice. So the motivation to give that advice by asking for advice is increasing. So a simple change there. Let me first go into step two, when we collect the evidence, show the evidence. And one example that I use always when I do my online info stocks is the example from my previous company. And it's for me the perfect example of making a mistake in showing the evidence. I saw this was from my kitchen business and the Dutch word is beordelinge in the top navigation. That means reviews, basically. But I hid it behind that button. I basically told my visitors, you find these reviews. That's wrong. We have to bring it to our clients that could be in our email signature on our webpage on our quotations in our presentations. We are responsible to show the evidence. Don't let your target of influence search for it. So when I changed that I put it on the homepage because I had over 10,000 reviews. I put it here about the call to action, which was book a free consultation to this to so we can design your kitchen. And that led to a 34.3% increase in leads. I had those reviews. I had them. They were on the website, but I didn't raise them to awareness. And this because the conversion from lead to actual visit to one of our shops to buying a kitchen almost stayed the same. It was also an increase of 3.1 million euros in revenue. It didn't cost me a single thing, but just when and how to present the social proof. So my big tip is focus on if you collected it, make you bring it to your clients. And another one that we did here where you see the 94% of our clients recommends us. That's an interesting one as well, because this is a social proof statement. But my tip for everybody that is going to collect or is collecting social proof. Always ask the question, will you recommend me. Why you trigger at least two principles that data you can use as social proof like we did here 94% of our clients recommend us. But what is the other principle that you trigger commitment and consistency. They just made a commitment. If they give that answer. Yes, I would recommend you. So here is an option to follow up. Well, you told me you would recommend me. Please do so. And then that's a new persuasion strategy, but you have your first commitment from somebody and we know from that principle that's powerful. Well, and at one moment even a third principle could kick in if that's a Warren Buffett says I would recommend you now becomes even authority. As you know, yeah, you want to make a comment here. Yeah, so, so boss, you know, this is, I found that pretty much every website that I've looked at that people try to use testimonials because they say, I do that I use testimonials. But they don't, they make it hard for people to find it. Yeah. And, and they think that people will search for them when they're on their website. When they really don't so the biggest recommendation I give people when they want to do that is that has to be the first per that that's that has to be the first thing people see when they come to your website. They need to see a testimonial they need to see a review score they need because that's the first thing they're looking for. And if they don't see it, they're not going to spend the time to find it. So I think your example just really prove that and the impact of that. Yeah, thank you. I'm bringing this to awareness and I want to even dive a bit deeper. Every time we search something online, which we don't search for often or we don't buy often, let's say the kitchen, how many times we buy a kitchen. So you never do this you Google about something about buying a kitchen, but bang, there are a million hits right away. I can click on all of them. What do I click subconsciously uncertainty kicks in right away. So how do we guide it already where to click nowadays with social proof because if you collect reviews you can connect that to your search results and you see in search advertising but also organic results in Google. You sometimes see these stars, what how people are rated. People click more often on search results who has those stars. And then by clicking what our brain really likes is confirmation that they landed on the correct page. So authority and social proof should indeed be above default. I click I'm still uncertain where I'm buying this kitchen. Well, I saw some stars there. I decided to click on it. If our brain gets confirmation that they are still on the right place, we keep on moving forward. So I completely agree. Social proof authority always should be above default in a landing page. It can be testimonials. It can be a summary of your score. It can be authority companies that you work for but some form of one of both should be there. So thanks for bringing that as you know. If you do also pay attention because it's just good information, which score is the most persuasive? And that's the score on a five point scale between 4.2, 4.7. Why is it not a five out of five stars? Because we think subconsciously nobody is that good that they never make a mistake or that all clients are super happy with them. So above it becomes less credible below 4.2. People just think it's not good enough. So be aware on what is your score. I don't say if you have only five star reviews. And ultimately, you would definitely say the top one everybody tells us tells we are the best five out of five stars. But from a persuasion point of view, this is the best call. And why that is because again, the credibility of everybody telling you are five out of five stars, not credible. Then nine out of 10 is, well, I don't really believe you did your homework. This sounds like guesswork. And 89% sounds you did your homework, you did your research, and now you are credible center of the social proof. And basically the lowest score here is the most persuasive. This is interesting to know. How do I create review summaries. And again, not only online, but you don't have a lot of space online above default. You cannot have you have also you need your message out there. So review summaries work. That's also social proof that gives us a soft landing that gives us that first certain to you and we land somewhere. And this doesn't cost a lot of space in your website, but think about how to do it in the offline world is similar. If you send out brochures, send out quotation printed elements. There is a place for these kind of statements. How much people recommend you? What is your review summary? But now one that we I think never thought is the timing of when people give you the reviews. And when I first saw that, that's very interesting. It has to do the research set as a statement. Positive reviews on the internet are considered just as valuable as negative ones. If the writers of the review reviews truthfully indicate that they describe the experience of that same day. In other words, if they had the experience the same day that they wrote a review, then positive reviews are equally valued as negative ones. People are drawn much more to negative reviews. That was the hypothesis. So what they did, they reviewed 65,000 reviews on Yelp. So Yelp is a big review platform. And they looked in five major US cities. These were reviews from the past three years they looked at. And it was about 100 restaurants. And on Yelp, you can say, do you consider this review helpful? And they looked what was the rating, negative or positive. And they looked for other presence of words that the review was written the same day as they visit the restaurant. And then they analyzed this. And what they found was indeed, when there was no indication of when the review was, we just found negative reviews more useful than positive reviews. The fact that a human being is drawn much more to negativity. But you don't want negativity about you being the top of mind. Positivity, of course. So how they could balance that when it was clear that the review were written on the same day the restaurant was visited. The reviews were valued equally as important. My tip here is, if you are for reviews or your clients, if people can write certain words, oh, I was today in a presentation. Or I thought, this morning, language like that, which suggests the experience happened on the same day that the person wrote the review, that could do that balance out the positivity versus the negativity. I thought there was very fascinating research about reviews on how subtle language can have a focus area for the next people who are reading your reviews. Well, then about statements. And this is a form of social proof, but it's how you can make people feel they are part of a group. So it's the suggested part of a group. So this is very subtle use of social proof. This is done by World Wildlife Foundation. And they had an email with a subject line become a WWF member and help protect vulnerable wildlife. That was, of course, to raise funds. Now they did something. They personalized it. First name in their newsletter, Adriana. And they knew she was from New York. If 90 more people from New York joined WWF, we could reach a membership month goal. Now suddenly you belong to a group where you, before this email was sent, you didn't feel associated with. But now suddenly there is a group in New York that needs to protect, have helped WWF. But that produced a 109% more open rate. These are subtle examples of social proof. And you could argue it's almost unity what they're trying to bring to awareness here. Why say it's social proof because there actually is no group. Then there's example. Last one. Some of you might notice, but I believe that when we, and that's why I repeat this. This is also one I like to discuss when I talk about online influence. But this is applicable offline, online, on web pages, email signatures, printed brochures. Again, it's about quotes, short language. So there's always room for it in a design, offline and online. There are very powerful directors of what the crowd is doing or what people like you are doing, which can produce great results. And I want to readdress how you guys can start making these sentences if you have the data. So NYX Makeup Studio, they had this design. You saw a product, a buy button. Very common. The first test was 71 beauties have viewed this product today, just below the buy button. Did it work? Yes, definitely. 33% more transactions on their web page. It was done with every product and it was data driven, so they made sure it was the truth. But then they thought, what is their desired behavior? What does NYX Makeup Studio wants people to do? Viewing products? No, purchasing products. So they changed 71 beauties. Purchase this product today. Why this was still ethical? Because if you, they purchased the product, so the purchase, you also have to view the product. It was of course the test. And that led to 100% increase in transactions. Changing one word and to go from 33% to 100, that's a 300% increase. Changing one word. Now we see subtle nuances and maybe Vladimir, your question. How can I persuade people to know it all? How can you use this example and say, which words you need to change to go from plus 33 to plus 100? Tell me and then I walk away. Maybe that's the case, but that could be bold or rude. So that has to really depend on the situation. Anyhow, how can you make these sentences? We go a few minutes over time. Sorry for that. First, describe your audience. Know to who you're talking to. So NYX said people that probably come to our Makeup Studio, they want to be a beauty. Another fictional example here says what you see on many websites. We have 12,000 followers. If you target business managers, you'd say 12,000 business managers are following me. Similar others. Find people that who you are talking to. And on different areas of your website, that could be different people, right? In one landing page, you might target sales professionals and in another one, sea level leaders. So adjust those. Be the slew that does his or her homework. And then this one, name the desired behavior literally. Guide people in what you want them to do. And we often think when we design especially in marketing, how do we please that visitor? We know also that visitor needs to be instructed. Tell them what your desired behavior is. In that case, what viewing versus purchasing. In my fictional example, I want the new business managers to subscribe to our channel. So I don't say anymore, this many people are following me. No, I say 12,000 business managers subscribe to our channel. It's not offensive language. You just raise to awareness. That's the goal. That's what you're supposed to do. And the last one exact numbers boost trust in the NYX makeup test was 71 not 70. We go from 12,000 to 12,000 34. So these three criteria, if you have the data, you analyze it, where are people or the majority of people, then it's a percentage. Doing what I want to do, raise that to awareness at different areas. You can do this printed and online. Last element of social proof, which you might be aware of, but another interesting one, if you don't have the support of the majority. And that's the common mistake I started with that we say 35% is already doing it. That's a mistake. But if 35% is doing it, and you have a trend that led to the 35%, maybe you have a new product, maybe you start with something new. For example, conserving energy. We could say in January, 10% of in our area started conserving energy. In April, it was 20% and today in July, it's already 30%. Now the projectory of that trend is filled in. And people, and still it's projected that it will become the majority. So people think I will start doing so as well. So this is pretty new research, which I always learned from Dr. Chaldini. And the key here is, so if you see from now on a minority, you don't say the minority number, you ask yourself one question. Is there a trend, increasing trend that led to this number? Now I can use it. What you need is at least three data points to prove a trend. And then you can bring that to awareness. So what we should do, we collect the evidence, then we share what multiple others and comparable others are doing. And we have a wide variety of ways how to do it.
